title: Systems Development and Agile
summary: Systems development lifecycle (SDLC): phases, stakeholders, requirements, and the Agile manifesto as an alternative to traditional waterfall approaches.
content_html:
Systems Development and Agile

Systems development is the entire set of activities needed to develop and deploy an IS/IT solution for a business problem or opportunity.

Stakeholders in Systems Development

  • End-Users: Concerned about a system that meets their needs, is easy to use, and is delivered as promised.
  • Owners: Concerned about business needs, value for money, budget, and timely delivery.
  • IS Developers: Face challenges from budget/time constraints, unclear requirements, and constantly changing technologies.

Types of Requirements

  • Functional requirements: What the system must do (e.g. update customer details, generate a daily report).
  • Non-functional requirements: Characteristics other than activities -- performance, security, data volumes, user access restrictions.
  • User requirements: From the user perspective -- usability, accessibility.
  • Business requirements: Strategic and operational goals the system must support.

SDLC (Systems Development Life Cycle) - Waterfall

SDLC is a phased project model with clear, distinct phases:

  1. Planning
  2. Requirements analysis
  3. System design
  4. Development/coding
  5. Testing
  6. Deployment and maintenance

Risks: Changes late in the cycle are expensive; users may not know what they want upfront; documentation-heavy; assumes requirements are stable.

Agile Development

Agile emerged in 2001 when practitioners frustrated by excessive SDLC planning created the Agile Manifesto:

  • Individuals and interactions over processes and tools.
  • Working software over comprehensive documentation.
  • Customer collaboration over contract negotiation.
  • Responding to change over following a plan.

Agile works through short iterations (sprints), frequent delivery of working software, and continuous stakeholder feedback. It is now applied broadly beyond software development to project management, architecture design, testing, requirements analysis, and many other fields.

Waterfall vs Agile

Waterfall is best for stable, well-defined requirements. Agile is best for complex, evolving requirements where early user feedback is valuable. Many organisations use hybrid approaches.

exercises.title: SDLC phases count
exercises.prompt: The traditional waterfall SDLC has how many core phases?

exercises.title: Functional requirement
exercises.prompt: Which is an example of a functional requirement?
options: The system must handle 10,000 concurrent users
The system must generate a daily transaction report
The system must be available 99.9% of the time
The system must encrypt all user passwords
exercises.answer: The system must generate a daily transaction report

exercises.title: Agile manifesto value
exercises.prompt: The Agile Manifesto values which of the following OVER processes and tools?
options: Comprehensive documentation
Contract negotiation
Individuals and interactions
Following the plan

exercises.title: Waterfall risk
exercises.prompt: A key risk of the waterfall SDLC approach is:
options: Too many iterations of the software
Insufficient documentation
Changes late in the cycle are expensive and requirements may not be stable
Too much customer involvement
exercises.answer: Changes late in the cycle are expensive and requirements may not be stable

exercises.title: Agile origin
exercises.prompt: Agile development was created by practitioners who were frustrated with:
options: Cloud computing limitations
Excessive planning and documentation in traditional SDLC
Too little documentation in software projects
High costs of software testing
exercises.answer: Excessive planning and documentation in traditional SDLC

exercises.title: Non-functional requirement
exercises.prompt: Which is an example of a non-functional requirement?
options: The system shall allow users to reset their passwords
The system shall process 500 transactions per second
The system shall display customer order history
The system shall send confirmation emails
exercises.answer: The system shall process 500 transactions per second

exercises.title: Agile iteration
exercises.prompt: In Agile development, work is organised into:
options: A single long development phase
Short iterations (sprints) with frequent delivery of working software
Annual release cycles
Sequential phases with gates between them
exercises.answer: Short iterations (sprints) with frequent delivery of working software

exercises.title: Waterfall vs Agile
exercises.prompt: Waterfall SDLC is most appropriate when:
options: Requirements are complex and likely to change frequently
The project is small and the team is co-located
Requirements are stable and well-defined upfront
The customer wants continuous involvement
exercises.answer: Requirements are stable and well-defined upfront
